q001.question: Clouds can be made up of:

options.B: the Ns give continuous rain and the Cb violent rain, lightning and strong turbulent winds.
options.C: the Ns give violent precipitation, the Cb continuous rain.

options.C: cumulus.
options.D: cirrocumulus, nimbostratus and stratocumulus.

q013.question: The atmosphere is made up of layers. The layer in which we live is called:

options.C: stratosphere.

q014.question: In meteorology, an air mass is characterized by:

options.C: the vertical distribution of temperature.

options.C: humidity.

options.C: remains constant.

options.C: probably the establishment of an inversion.

options.C: evaporation/condensation and turbulence.

q022.question: The sun heats the atmosphere mainly:

options.C: indirectly by means of the ground.
radiation.

q027.question: A wind of 20 kt (knots) blows at:

options.C: 20 km/h.

q030.question: The following situation is called temperature inversion:

options.A: a layer of cold air is above a layer of warm air.
options.B: a layer of warm air is above a layer of cold air.
options.C: a layer of air blocks the development of updrafts.

options.D: stratus tend to develop.

options.C: the warmer air infiltrates beneath the colder air.

options.C: can be sent downward or lifted depending on the situation.

q034.question: The general tendencies of a cold front are:

options.C: the air is very humid and the cloud system is significant.

options.C: increases progressively up to the stall.

q070.question: On a wing in stabilized straight flight, when the pilot causes a decrease in incidence:

options.C: the trajectory curves downward.
options.D: the speed decreases.

q114.question: The glide ratio is higher when:

q115.question: The glide ratio over the ground:

q116.question: The glide ratio over the ground:

q117.question: The glide ratio over the ground:

options.A: the wing loading, the glide ratio over the ground.
options.B: the maximum glide ratio, the minimum sink rate, the penetration.
options.C: the handling, the behavior in turbulence.
options.D: the stability.

q184.question: For your safety you have the wing checked by the manufacturer when you notice:

options.C: damaged seams.

q185.question: Your new wing systematically turns to one side, without braking and with the brake lines extended:

options.A: you lengthen the brake on the opposite side.
options.B: you change the level of the risers on the harness.
options.C: you loosen the leg strap on the opposite side.
options.D: you contact the dealer for a factory check and/or an instructor or experienced pilot.

options.A: decreases the penetration of the wing.
options.C: makes the wing slow to inflate.
options.D: makes the wing more nervous to take off.

options.A: increases the penetration of the wing.
options.C: makes the wing slower to inflate.
options.D: makes the wing more nervous to take off.

q188.question: In-flight trim adjustment systems (trims, speed bars):

options.C: must be used with caution in turbulence, especially during the adjustment process.
options.D: must be able to be released easily in flight.

q189.question: During storage and transport, the paraglider must be protected from proximity to or contact with:

options.C: moisture.
options.D: the sun.

q190.question: List the factors that age a wing:

options.C: turbulence.

options.A: can prevent the wing from rising during inflation.
options.B: reduce the ability to gain speed in flight if needed.
options.C: increase the risk of stall.
options.D: are favorable especially in the case of beginners.

q192.question: Each line withstands a weight on the order of:

options.C: with polyester webbing.

options.A: has a low percentage of stretch.
options.C: has good tensile strength, but is weak under shear and bending.
options.D: must be sheathed for effective protection against abrasion and ultraviolet rays.

options.B: has a low percentage of stretch.
options.C: has a low melting temperature (significant risk of breakage from friction).
options.D: has good tensile strength.

options.B: can reduce tensile strength by up to 50%.
options.C: has no influence on tensile strength.

options.A: to promote the practice of free flight in competition.
options.C: to advance equipment technology.
options.D: to provide a French certification for free-flight wings.

q198.question: The certification sticker on a paraglider:

options.A: is not mandatory on every unit of a certified model.
options.B: gives indications about the behavior of that model in certain flight configurations.
options.C: allows you to get an idea of the piloting level required for that model (beginner, intermediate...)
options.D: indicates which type of harness the tests were performed with.

options.A: certifies that any certified paraglider can be used without risk by any pilot (even beginners).
options.B: allows you to get an idea of the behavior of that model.
options.C: requires the manufacturer to maintain the technical characteristics across all units produced.
options.D: is valid regardless of the harness mounted on the wing.

options.C: an aircraft not subject to registration.
options.D: a parachute.

options.A: Ultralight free-flight aircraft (P.U.L.) have right-of-way over gliders.
options.B: ultralight free-flight aircraft (P.U.L.) must give way to gliders.
options.C: ultralight free-flight aircraft (P.U.L.) have right-of-way over towed combinations (airplane + glider, microlight + hang glider, microlight + advertising banner, etc...).

options.A: the hang glider must give way to the paraglider.
options.B: the paraglider must give way to the hang glider.
options.C: hang glider and paraglider must observe the same right-of-way rules in the air.

q203.question: A wing is coming head-on toward you; you perform:

options.A: a turn to the left (the other also turns to the left).
options.B: a turn to the right (the other also turns to the right).
options.C: a descent (the other stays at the same level).

q204.question: In flight, an ultralight free-flight aircraft (P.U.L.) is coming from your right on a converging course:

options.C: right-of-way depends on the size of the aircraft.

q205.question: Away from the terrain, you are following the same course and at the same level as a slower wing; you overtake it on:

options.C: the right.

q206.question: Flying at the same altitude, right-of-way belongs to:

options.A: the wing arriving from the right.
options.C: the wing that reaches the lift first.
options.D: the wing piloted by a beginner.

q207.question: In ridge soaring, right-of-way belongs to the wing that:

options.A: has the slope on its right.
options.B: has the slope on its left.
options.C: is located windward of the terrain.
options.D: is located leeward of the terrain.

q208.question: You are ridge soaring, with the terrain on your left. A wing comes head-on at the same altitude:

options.B: you have right-of-way and maintain your heading.
options.C: you try to pass it underneath.

q209.question: Two wings are following each other near terrain located on their right; the second wing is faster:

options.A: it must overtake on the left.
options.B: it must overtake on the right, between the terrain and the other wing.
options.C: it turns back.

q210.question: You are the first to enter a thermal:

options.A: wings arriving afterward must adopt your turn direction.
options.B: a wing entering the thermal at the same level as you must turn 180 degrees and in the same direction.
options.C: you have right-of-way over a wing located below that is overtaking you.
options.D: no other wing has the right to use this thermal.

q211.question: You want to enter a thermal while another wing is circling 50 m below:

options.A: you turn in the same direction as the other wing.
options.B: you turn in the opposite direction so you can keep watch on it.
options.C: you wait for it to pass above you before entering, since otherwise the peripheral sink may bring it to your same altitude.

options.A: more than 300 meters above any terrain.
options.B: more than 900 meters above sea level.
options.C: nowhere.

q213.question: Overflying isolated obstacles must be done with a minimum vertical and horizontal clearance of:

options.C: 150 meters.
options.D: 200 meters.

q214.question: Overflying built-up areas or a gathering of people must be done with a minimum clearance of:

options.C: 300 meters vertically and 600 meters horizontally.

q215.question: The flight rules require each pilot:

options.A: to keep a sufficient distance to prevent collisions.
options.B: to keep to the right in case of a crossing at the same altitude.
options.C: to pass a slower wing on the left.
options.D: to turn in the same direction as the first wing that enters the thermal lift.

q216.question: The care of the flying sites concerns:

options.C: the Ministry of the Interior.
options.D: each pilot.

options.A: avoiding the accumulation of stress factors that individually do not lead to accidents but, added together, make the risk unacceptable.
options.B: measuring the risks and not adding them.
options.C: identifying the new elements of a situation and accepting only one at a time.

options.A: is due to an oxygen deficit in the blood.
options.B: is due to the cooling of the atmosphere.
options.C: can manifest as a feeling of well-being.
options.D: causes impaired judgment and a decrease in muscular coordination.

options.A: the first symptoms can appear at around 2000 m above sea level.
options.C: can impair judgment.
options.D: has different effects on each pilot.

q220.question: Factors that aggravate the risk of hypoxia:

options.C: a diet high in fat.
options.D: a poorly dressed (insufficiently warm) pilot.

q221.question: Anticipating the possibility of gaining a lot of altitude or a prolonged flight, it is advisable to equip yourself with:

options.C: good footwear, warm clothing, gloves, sunglasses, drinking water.

q222.question: In strong wind, tethered flight over a fixed point (line tied to the ground):

options.C: is not advisable.
options.D: can cause situations in which the wing becomes uncontrollable.

q223.question: When establishing a flight plan, the following is taken into account:

options.A: the location of the take-off and landing areas.
options.C: the wind at altitude.
options.D: the valley wind.

q224.question: To take off safely, it is better to choose a slope:

options.C: without obstacles.

q225.question: The inclination of a take-off area must be:

options.A: less than or equal to the inclination of the wing's maximum glide ratio.
options.B: greater than the inclination of the wing's maximum glide ratio.
options.C: it should not be compared with the inclination of the wing's glide ratio.

q226.question: You are preparing to take off at 3 p.m. from a sunlit slope where a light mountain breeze is setting in. The meteorological wind is nil:

options.A: the wind will be nil or very weak in the valley.
options.B: you must anticipate the possibility of strong wind in the valley, especially if it is narrow.
options.C: in all cases it is prudent to anticipate the possibility of alternate landings, taking into account possible strong wind in the valley.

q227.question: Your take-off faces west, and the weather is good. To find the best launch conditions, it is better to take off:

options.B: the time of day does not matter.
options.C: in the early afternoon.

q228.question: Your take-off faces east, and the weather is good. To find the best conditions, it is better to take off:

options.B: the time of day does not matter.
options.C: during the afternoon.

q229.question: You are in the mountains, there is no wind, and in theory, with the wing's glide ratio, you would reach the landing field exactly. You:

options.C: fly at midday to take advantage of the lift.
options.D: the ground effect will help you on landing.

q230.question: A stratus layer 40 meters thick is located below the take-off:

options.A: 40 m is little, you take off.
options.B: you wait for it to clear, otherwise you do not take off.
options.C: if there is enough wind for ridge soaring, you take off and stay above the layer.

q231.question: Compared with the wind measured at take-off 2 meters above the ground, there is a risk that 30 meters higher up it will be:

options.C: weaker.

options.B: a good run and that's it.
options.C: a crosswind, but not very strong, is not a problem.
options.D: you look for a better-oriented take-off.

q233.question: During the take-off run, if the slope of the ground increases and the trim does not change, the angle of attack:

options.C: increases.

q234.question: During the take-off run, your angle of attack is the angle between the plane of the wing and:

options.C: the horizontal.

q235.question: During take-off the wind is from behind. The chances of not getting airborne are due to:

options.B: the effects of the wind gradient.
options.C: the high speed you would have to reach while running.

q236.question: On take-off there is 20 km/h over the slope, your wing flies starting from 30 km/h; on your run to take off you must run at:

options.C: 30 km/h.
options.D: 40 km/h.

q237.question: In high mountains, air density decreases with altitude, and for my wing to be able to take off, I must run:

options.C: for longer.
options.D: for less time.

q238.question: After a take-off run with the harness brushing the ground, you barely manage to take off:

options.A: you are surely in fast flight regime, you can reduce speed to enter minimum sink rate.
options.B: you are in slow flight regime and you must skim the terrain gaining speed to switch to normal flight regime.
options.C: you are probably in danger; if you do not immediately gain speed/airflow, you may fall.

q239.question: A friend has just taken off, you catch up with him to fly together:

options.A: it is dangerous because you will encounter the turbulence of his wing.
options.B: it is the only way to take good photos.
options.C: it is delicate but possible.

q240.question: The turbulence caused by another wing is dangerous:

options.B: over a few tens of meters behind in its path/airflow.
options.C: because it can cause collapses on your own wing.

q241.question: The turbulence of a wing is dangerous for another aircraft located:

options.C: below.
options.D: in the path/airflow.

q242.question: Flying while ensuring a landing place means:

options.A: having an endurance that allows you to reach it at all times regardless of the aerological conditions encountered.
options.B: staying over the usual site without covering distance.

q243.question: In case the meteo wind changes orientation or intensity during the flight, it is advisable to:

options.A: change the flight plan in time to have a better chance of a safe landing.
options.B: immediately set course toward the terrain to return at all costs.
options.C: decelerate to the maximum to delay the moment of landing, which will be delicate.
options.D: immediately locate alternative terrains to prepare for the eventuality of an emergency landing.

q244.question: A wing flies into the wind and advances relative to the ground; the wind is:

options.C: impossible to know.

q245.question: You fly with a tailwind, your cruise airspeed is equal to the meteo wind speed; the ground:

options.A: passes by twice as fast as with no wind.
options.B: passes by twice as slow as with no wind.
options.C: appears fixed.

q246.question: The speed of your aircraft relative to the ground is:

options.C: it never changes.

q247.question: When you fly with a tailwind close to the ground, the visual impression tends to alter the perception of airspeed, and it can happen that without realizing it you:

options.C: enter a stall.

q248.question: If you fly into a meteo wind, to maintain the maximum glide ratio over ground, relative to the speed corresponding to the maximum glide ratio in the air, you must:

options.C: look for the lowest sink rate.

q249.question: To maintain a maximum glide ratio over ground, you must fly faster than at the maximum airspeed glide ratio speed:

options.C: with a headwind.
options.D: with a tailwind.

q250.question: You cross head-on with another wing; your closing speed is equal to:

options.B: the sum of the two airspeeds.
options.C: half of your airspeed.

q251.question: You cross with another wing; you must calculate and react:

options.C: with a headwind you have more time than usual.

q252.question: Your path over ground is perpendicular to the wind direction:

options.A: the central chord of the wing is perpendicular to the wind direction.
options.B: you are necessarily in a sideslip.
options.C: you are drifting.

options.A: the central chord of the wing aligns with the wind direction; this is the weathervane effect.
options.B: you must adopt a counter-drift angle if you are heading toward a point that is not on the wind axis.
options.C: the central chord of the wing forms an angle with the path over ground.
options.D: the central chord of the wing remains parallel to the path over ground.

q254.question: Mark the two elements that define the drift angle:

options.B: the direction of the meteo wind.
options.C: the direction of the relative wind.
options.D: the path over ground.

q255.question: Strong dangerous turbulence can be encountered:

options.A: on the lee side of a relief with strong wind.
options.C: in zones of ridge lift.

q256.question: In turbulent conditions it is better to:

options.A: not perform maneuvers that require a lot of precision.
options.C: not get close to the limits of the piloting variables.

q257.question: In turbulent regime it is better to:

options.A: not perform maneuvers that require a lot of precision.
options.B: not play with speeds that are too low.
options.C: not play with speeds that are too high.

options.A: the stresses on the structure increase with flight speed.
options.B: you must fly as slowly as possible so as not to fatigue the structure.
options.C: you must fly as fast as possible to leave the zone.

q259.question: You are covering a part of the course where the wind is well across your route. You preferentially choose the cumulus clouds:

options.A: windward of the route to follow.
options.B: leeward of the route to follow.
options.C: on the route to follow.

q260.question: Before performing a turn, you must:

options.A: evaluate the loss of height due to the turn and the new path.
options.B: verify that the zone where you will be after the turn is clear.
options.C: go slower.

options.A: turn with your back to the slope.
options.C: pay attention to the wind gradient near the relief.
options.D: keep a sufficient speed margin to be able to move away from the slope in case of emergency.

q262.question: In ridge flying, to make better use of the lift, you fly at the angle of attack:

options.C: of maximum speed.
options.D: of minimum speed.

options.A: toward the side where the wing lifts.
options.C: toward the side opposite the relief.

q264.question: In ridge flying, turning toward the slope is:

options.C: it is difficult to control and dangerous because of the fast passing of the ground with a tailwind.
options.D: it is the one that imposes the strongest bank for the same turn radius relative to the ground.

options.B: constant observation of the evolution of the wind.
options.C: being accustomed to the drift effects imposed by the wind.

q266.question: During ridge flying in a turbulent atmosphere:

options.A: you avoid descending below the ridge edges.
options.B: you fly a little farther from the relief and increase the speed.
options.C: you fly as fast as possible.

q267.question: When a pilot uses a thermal bubble to gain height:

options.A: he rises at the same speed as the bubble.
options.B: he enters the bubble at its lower part and exits at the top.
options.C: he continuously descends inside the bubble.

q268.question: You manage to position yourself beneath a large cumulus, you observe some wispy clouds and a strongly positive variometer reading; you:

options.B: increase speed to bring the variometer to zero.
options.C: accelerate to head toward the clearer side, escaping the influence of the cloud.
options.D: look for the sink on the windward side of the cloud.

q269.question: During a spiral your aircraft drifted 3 km in 10 min. The wind strength is:

options.C: 18 km/h

q270.question: During a spiral your aircraft drifted 3 km in 10 min. The wind strength is:

options.C: 18 km/h

q271.question: You approach the base of a cumulus:

options.A: there is no risk of suction.
options.B: you may be sucked up by the lift, which tends to become stronger.
options.C: the risk is lower as long as the cloud is not of the congestus or nimbus type.
options.D: the risk worsens if the base is below the highest ridge edges.

q272.question: On a fine day of rising air on which storms have been forecast, you must be concerned if:

options.C: the shadows of the clouds on the ground grow larger quickly.

q273.question: A large isolated cumulus is always harmless:

options.A: false, it can have lift greater than your escape capabilities and turn into a cumulonimbus.
options.B: false, it can hide other aircraft.
options.C: true.

q274.question: The passage of a cumulonimbus through your flight sector:

options.A: invites you to make use of the lift before the rains.
options.C: can make landing problematic.

q275.question: After a long-duration flight, it is advisable to relax and prepare your arms and legs for the landing run:

options.C: on final.
options.D: by doing some aerial stunts during the approach.

q276.question: You have a 10 km/h headwind to return to the landing field:

options.A: you try to stay at minimum sink rate to have time to complete the route.
options.B: you fly as fast as possible to have maximum penetration speed.
options.C: taking reference points and managing speed, you try to find the best glide angle.

q277.question: An unknown landing field must be reconnoitered:

options.A: in flight, positioning yourself directly above to observe it.
options.C: at the moment of landing.

q278.question: You are heading toward the landing field with a tailwind at cruise speed; you see the nearest edges of the field rising relative to you:

options.A: you are going to arrive just at the field and land with a tailwind.
options.B: you are going to arrive over the field, make a U-turn and land into the wind.
options.C: you choose a better-located alternate landing.
options.D: you reduce speed to use the better glide ratio over ground due to the tailwind.

q279.question: You are on your approach, there is a wing below you:

options.A: you watch it, waiting for it, since you must give it priority.
options.B: you have priority over the other wing.
options.C: you do a few tight spirals to land sooner.
options.D: you carry out your flight, since on approach there are no priorities.

options.B: takes into account the meteo wind and its possible variations.
options.C: must be able to be modified if necessary.

q281.question: A good approach implies a final:

options.A: into the wind at the end of the field.
options.B: into the wind at the entrance of the field.
options.C: it all depends on the conditions.

options.A: allow you to be precise on landing.
options.C: make you lose sight of the reference points.
options.D: expose you to the risk of collision with the other wings that are approaching.

options.A: a reconnaissance at a good altitude (wind on the ground, obstacles, planned pattern...).
options.B: S-turns to assess the conditions and to be precise.
options.C: a final turn not too close to the ground and a stabilized segment on final.

q284.question: You are making an S-approach; your trajectory is sinking relative to the planned descent path, you must:

options.A: reduce the amplitude of the S-turns.
options.B: increase the radius of the turns.
options.C: if needed, fly in a straight line toward the landing field.
options.D: speed up so as not to lose time.

q285.question: To be precise in the landing, correctly visualizing the slope of the trajectory on final is essential. This slope:

options.A: varies as a function of the angle of attack.
options.B: varies as a function of the direction and speed of the wind.
options.C: does not depend on the aerodynamic characteristics of the wing.

q286.question: You are on final, facing the landing field, and you realize you are a bit long and high and may overshoot. You choose to:

options.A: lose altitude by putting the wing into a slight parachutal stall.
options.C: increase the distance to be covered by changing course.
options.D: increase the parasitic drag by sitting up.

q287.question: On final, if the target you have set descends in your field of vision, you are:

options.C: you must slow down as much as possible.

options.B: is necessary to keep a reserve of airspeed in case of a wind gradient.
options.C: is dangerous since the craft will climb when braking.
options.D: is necessary to improve maneuverability near the ground.

options.B: is dangerous; it can lead to a stall situation.
options.C: increases the sink rate.
options.D: makes building up speed necessary for safety.

q290.question: The final braking in a landing with strong wind:

options.C: requires precise dosing.

options.B: obeys the same laws of the air and the same demands as the other air sports.
options.C: forgives most piloting errors since it is practiced on a parachute.
options.D: requires serious training when used as a glider (performance flying) and light training in para-alpinism (to descend from mountains).

options.A: is done on the first or second day of the course after inflating the paraglider a couple of times.
options.B: is done directly, due to the risks of accident on the training slope.
options.C: requires serious technical training.
options.D: requires a gradual adaptation to the void.

q293.question: Obtaining the paragliding pilot license requires a progression:

options.C: that includes a minimum of 40 significant flights.
options.D: that ends with the purchase of your first wing.

q294.question: When you receive a new wing, you must:

options.A: first test it with one or two inflations on flat ground to check the brakes.
options.B: then check the wing's performance at a known flying site.
options.C: add a carabiner on the risers to make gripping easier.

q295.question: The best profile for a take-off area is one where the pilot can:

options.A: move away from the terrain quickly.
options.B: run easily down the slope to reach flying speed.
options.C: run little so as to launch into flight quickly.
options.D: stop in case of problems with the inflation.

options.A: is made easier by the lower air density.
options.B: is para-alpinism and not free flight.
options.C: requires a high level of experience.
options.D: does not require any particular knowledge of mountaineering.

options.B: requires running, at least at the wing's minimum sink rate speed.
options.C: requires significant control with the brakes.

q298.question: At take-off there is 30 km/h of wind, my wing flies at a maximum speed of 35 km/h:

options.A: I must run at 5 km/h to take off.
options.B: I do not take off because I consider the safety margin insufficient.
options.C: I pull on the front risers as soon as I have taken off.

q299.question: At take-off, with 20 km/h headwind, the wing can be inflated:

options.B: facing the slope, having another person secure us so as not to be pulled back at the moment the wing rises.
options.C: with the back to the slope, the pilot turning around to take off.

q300.question: Inflating while facing the wing allows you to take off:

options.A: without risk in a wind above 30 km/h.
options.C: it allows controlling the wing easily while it rises.
options.D: without any assistance in all cases.

q301.question: At take-off the wind is crosswind (30 degrees, 3 m/s):

options.A: You inflate facing into the wind, build up speed and progressively orient the run to face the slope.
options.B: you run along the line of maximum slope, pulling on the front risers.

q302.question: 30 km/h of headwind at take-off, you are alone:

options.A: it is too much, you refrain from flying.
options.B: an experienced pilot can take off without problems with the back to the wind.
options.C: you do some inflation tests since either you will not be able to do it or the wing will come up on its own without the need to run.

q303.question: Laying out the paraglider on the ground depends:

options.A: on the strength and direction of the wind.
options.C: on the characteristics of the paraglider.

q304.question: The paragliding take-off is made up of:

options.A: inflation of the wing (acceleration and looking forward).
options.B: stabilization and control of the wing (timing and looking at the wing).
options.C: take-off (acceleration and looking forward).

q305.question: The stabilization and control of the wing:

options.A: is necessary to check the whole wing and detect any knots in the lines.
options.B: requires a momentary halt of the acceleration.
options.C: is necessary to verify that the aircraft you have just built is fit to fly.

q306.question: The acceleration phase after stabilization requires:

options.A: leaning forward for an effective build-up of speed.
options.B: leaning forward to avoid loss of balance.
options.C: leaning backward to control the wing better.

q307.question: During an inflation with strong wind, the wing begins to pull you backward very strongly; you risk being dragged:

options.A: you release the brakes and hold on to whatever you can to support yourself.
options.B: you pull both brakes all the way to stop yourself.
options.C: you control the wing by pulling on one rear riser and moving toward it.
options.D: you feather the wing with the help of the rear risers.

q308.question: During an inflation the wing positions itself in a crescent shape, you must:

options.B: pull the brakes, then hold the front risers.
options.C: stop and start again.
options.D: pull on the front risers.

q309.question: After inflation, your wing has an inflation defect on the right and veers to the right:

options.A: you run more slowly and pull hard on the right brake.
options.B: you brake vigorously on the right, then turn left.
options.C: you follow the wing until correcting the trajectory using the brakes, then pull on the brakes to finish inflating it.

q310.question: During stabilization, your wing stays to the left:

options.A: You correct by moving to the right to recover the wing.
options.B: you follow the wing by moving to the left, braking gently on the right to bring it back to the line of maximum slope.
options.C: you maintain speed by pulling on the front risers.
options.D: you accelerate and brake on the left.

q311.question: During stabilization you have several left lateral cells closed:

options.A: you pull the right brake and slow down.
options.B: you pull the left front riser to give more speed to that side.
options.C: you maintain speed and try to re-inflate with a gentle movement of the brakes.
options.D: you do not worry, they always end up opening on their own.

q312.question: On the ground your wing veers off the planned trajectory:

options.A: You correct by bringing the wing back with your body and braking on the side toward which you want to bring it.
options.B: you follow the wing to stay in the center and bring it back by braking on the side where you want to go.
options.C: You correct with the front risers.

q313.question: Your wing is inflated, stabilized. To take off:

options.A: you keep accelerating while holding the front risers firmly.
options.B: you accelerate with your hands up until take-off.
options.C: you accelerate with your hands up and brake at the moment of take-off.
options.D: you accelerate, placing the brakes at the level of the cruise flight setting.

options.A: release the brakes to sit down better.
options.B: cannot pilot if you are poorly seated.
options.C: visually check that the paraglider is well inflated.
options.D: attend to your trajectory and to the other pilots who are flying.

q315.question: Using the front risers in flight:

options.A: is not very useful aerodynamically because the wing sinks more than what is gained in penetration.
options.C: causes more problems than solutions.

q316.question: Pulling on the front risers in flight:

options.C: can cause a collapse of the wing.

q317.question: Flying with your arms up, the brakes are taut and the trailing edge is a bit low:

options.A: this improves the glide ratio with no action on the brakes.
options.B: it has little influence on the wing.
options.C: it is better for inflation.
options.D: the brakes should be lengthened and adjusted for the next flight.

q318.question: While flying, if you release the brakes:

options.B: the wing flies at maximum speed.
options.C: they must never be released.

options.A: while keeping the brakes in your hands.
options.C: by symmetrically pulling one or several outer front lines (read the technical instructions for your wing).

q320.question: When you make big ears you:

options.C: increase the maneuverability of the wing.

q321.question: When you make big ears you:

options.B: with a headwind your ground glide ratio increases.
options.C: descend more quickly in safety.
options.D: do not damage the wing.

q322.question: In turbulence, you make big ears:

options.A: your wing will be more stable.
options.B: your wing will be more sensitive.
options.C: it is not advised.

options.A: is a form of rapid descent.
options.C: damages the anchor points of this line of suspensions.
options.D: can cause a stabilized parachutal stall situation.

q324.question: At minimum sink rate speed, to begin a right turn without losing too much speed and the consequent risk of entering a flat spin, you must:

options.C: raise the left brake.

options.A: must not be done when landing.
options.B: can be vigorous because the risk of a stall is nil.
options.C: is made easier with an action on the harness seat.
options.D: must be done more banked the narrower the thermal is.

options.C: allows assessing the drift.

q327.question: After a piloting error, you notice the almost total cessation of the relative wind. The wing sinks. To return to the normal flight regime as quickly as possible you must:

options.B: progressively pull the front risers to decrease the angle of attack.
options.C: gently release the brakes to dampen the surge.

q328.question: Entering a stall can be dangerous:

options.C: in turbulent conditions.

q329.question: To recover from a symmetric stall:

options.A: wait for the wing to open on its own.
options.B: give sharp jerks to the front risers.
options.C: give sharp jerks to the brakes.
options.D: gently release the brakes and dampen the surge.

q330.question: You are flying at minimum sink rate; you are about to enter a thermal:

options.C: you get ready to release the brakes.
options.D: you increase your speed.

q331.question: To stay in ridge lift close to the terrain, you:

options.A: make 360-degree turns in front of the slope.
options.B: pull the brakes down a lot to fly slowly.
options.C: make long "figure 8s" turning into the wind.
options.D: make long "figure 8s" turning toward the slope.

q332.question: While flying, if you feel turbulence:

options.A: you pull on the front risers.
options.C: you fly a little slower than at cruise regime.

q333.question: In turbulent flight, to minimize the risks of auto-rotation and of turning toward the slope, you must fly:

options.C: between minimum sink rate and maximum glide ratio.

q334.question: During ridge flight, a violent turbulence collapses 50% of the wing:

options.A: you raise your arms and gain speed to avoid entering a stall.
options.B: you observe the nature of the terrain beneath your feet and get ready for a forced landing.
options.C: you control your trajectory to avoid turning toward the slope, maintaining sufficient speed to avoid entering a stall or auto-rotation.
options.D: you pull the brake corresponding to the closed side in order to reopen it.

q335.question: During a ridge flight 50 m above the ground, you suddenly sink toward the terrain and the whistle of the relative wind decreases. To avoid the impact:

options.A: you brake immediately to better control the wing.
options.B: you calmly release the brakes and turn when speed is sufficient to move away from the slope.
options.C: the situation is unrecoverable, you get ready to land on the slope.

q336.question: Your wing deflates abruptly on the side toward the terrain:

options.A: you reinflate it energetically, avoiding turning toward the terrain.
options.B: you first try to move away from the terrain while keeping sufficient speed before executing the appropriate maneuver to reinflate the wing.
options.C: you do nothing, the wing reinflates automatically.

q337.question: You have your back to the terrain and several cells collapse on the left side of the wing, causing you to turn left:

options.A: if the paraglider has ACPUL certification, you let it recover on its own.
options.B: you brake hard on the left to reinflate the closed side.
options.C: you brake symmetrically on both sides.
options.D: you correct to the right to move away from the terrain, keeping sufficient speed.

q338.question: In turbulent flight, your wing collapses at the front over most of the leading edge; you:

options.C: raise your hands to achieve maximum speed.

options.A: you release the brakes to accelerate and reinflate.
options.B: you brake on the opposite side to stabilize the trajectory, keeping sufficient speed.
options.C: you shift your weight to the right side of the harness.
options.D: you make smooth, wide movements with the left brake once the trajectory is stabilized.

q340.question: Your wing is in a parachutal stall; the usual maneuvers to exit are:

options.B: pulling both brakes to put it transiently into a stall.
options.C: pulling both front risers.
options.D: if it is close to the ground, you get ready for a forced landing without doing anything, since any of the previous maneuvers will throw you forward, gaining speed.

q341.question: The risk of entering a flat spin:

options.A: increases by pulling one brake down hard in slow flight.
options.B: increases by pulling one brake down hard in fast flight.
options.C: varies according to the type of wing.

q342.question: To exit a flat spin, you must:

options.A: smoothly raise the brakes, counteracting on the outer side.
options.B: brake on the outer side of the turn and pull the front riser on the inner side.

q343.question: While flying, your right brake breaks; you can pilot with:

options.A: the left brake and the right rear riser.
options.B: the left brake and the right front riser.
options.C: both rear risers (symmetry of sensations).
options.D: the front risers to land quickly.

options.C: can be compensated for by piloting with the rear risers.

q345.question: You are forced to land on sloping terrain:

options.A: you always land against the slope.
options.B: if the slope is steep, you land into the wind.
options.C: if the slope is gentle, you land into the wind.
options.D: if the slope is steep, you land sideways to the slope.

q346.question: You are on final facing the landing field and realize you are a bit high and long, possibly overshooting the field:

options.A: you progressively brake the wing to the point of parachutal stall.
options.B: you lose altitude by making a 360-degree turn.
options.C: you lengthen the path by making a small "S".
options.D: you pull and release the brakes several times to shake the wing and reduce the glide ratio.

options.B: is needed to keep a good airspeed since the wind decreases as you approach the ground.
options.C: is dangerous because the craft will climb during the final braking.
options.D: makes the braking action more effective.

q348.question: To land smoothly when there is no wind:

options.A: you fly the entire final phase with the brakes at hip level.
options.C: you pull the brakes all the way down upon entering the field.
options.D: you gain speed at the beginning of the final phase.

q349.question: At 5 m above the ground you realize you are going to land with a crosswind:

options.A: you perform the final braking normally without trying to turn.
options.B: you try to turn to face into the wind.
options.C: you pull the brakes all the way down to correct.

q350.question: On final, at 30 m above the ground, you realize you are going to land with a tailwind:

options.A: you get ready to brake energetically and to run.
options.B: you make a 180-degree turn to correct.
options.C: you pull the brakes to enter a parachutal stall.

q351.question: There is 20 km/h of wind at the landing; you prepare:

options.A: to pull the brakes all the way down upon touching down.
options.B: to brake gently and accompany the wing by walking backward during its descent.
options.C: to pull the front risers to brake gently.
options.D: to turn around after landing and hold one or both rear risers so as not to be dragged.

options.A: you must watch the wing to make sure it does not close.
options.B: you must greatly reduce the speed.
options.C: landing in a turn is discouraged since it increases the sink rate and the pilot's lateral swing.
options.D: you must increase the speed on final before braking.

q353.question: The approach circuit at landing serves:

options.A: only as training for later passing the exams organized by the F.A.V.L.
options.B: exclusively beginners as an aid maneuver during the first landings.
options.C: as with other aircraft, as a procedure that offers the pilot the maximum chances of a precise landing.
options.D: according to the zone chosen for the loss of altitude and depending on the direction of turn, to indicate to approaching pilots which type of circuit has been chosen for landing.

q354.question: The part of the approach circuit indicated by the number 1 in the diagram is called:

options.C: initial.
options.D: final.

q355.question: The part of the approach circuit indicated by the number 2 in the diagram is called:

options.C: initial.
options.D: final.

q356.question: The part of the approach circuit indicated by the number 3 in the diagram is called:

options.C: initial.
options.D: final.

q357.question: The part of the approach circuit indicated by the number 4 in the diagram is called:

options.C: initial.
options.D: final.

q358.question: When arriving too high at the landing zone, and before beginning the initial phase, you always lose that altitude:

options.C: with turns in the same direction as the chosen approach circuit.
options.D: by making "S" turns.

q359.question: You have decided to make an approach circuit to the left. The loss of excess altitude before the initial phase must be done in the quadrant:

options.C: lower right.
options.D: lower left.

q360.question: You have decided to make an approach circuit to the right. The loss of excess altitude before the initial phase must be done in the quadrant:

options.C: lower right.
options.D: lower left.

q361.question: The following four diagrams show the type of standard approach with zero wind (dotted line), and a circuit corrected and adapted to the situation arising at the last moment in the reality of a landing (solid line). On the field, the wind is indeed nil, and at the moment of transitioning from the initial phase to base you realize that you are actually a bit below the ideal altitude. The modification you make is the one indicated in figure:

options.C: c.
options.D: d.

q362.question: The following four diagrams show the type of standard approach with zero wind (dotted line), and a circuit corrected and adapted to the situation arising at the last moment in the reality of a landing (solid line). On the field, the wind is indeed nil, and at the moment of transitioning from the initial phase to base you realize that you are actually a bit above the ideal altitude. The modification you make is the one indicated in figure:

options.C: c.
options.D: d.

q363.question: The following 4 diagrams show the type of standard approach with zero wind (dotted line), and a circuit corrected and adapted to the situation arising at the last moment in the reality of a landing (solid line). On the field, the wind is 10 to 15 km/h. The modification you make is the one indicated in figure:

options.C: c.
options.D: d.

q364.question: The following 4 diagrams show the type of standard approach with zero wind (dotted line), and a circuit corrected and adapted to the situation arising at the last moment in the reality of a landing (solid line). On the field, the wind is 25 to 30 km/h. The modification you make is the one indicated in figure:

options.C: c.
options.D: d.

q365.question: You arrive first at the landing zone, followed at 50 m horizontal distance by two other pilots. The wind is nil, there are no obstacles at any of the boundaries of the field, and there are no specific landing rules for the site. Given that you are the first pilot:

options.A: you may choose a circuit to the right or left and with the final phase in any direction, since the wind is nil.
options.B: you may freely choose the direction of the final phase, but you must make an approach circuit to the left.
options.C: you may freely choose an approach to the left or right, but the final phase must be transverse to the axis of the valley.
options.D: you must make an approach circuit to the left, but the final phase must be transverse to the axis of the valley.

q366.question: You arrive first at the landing zone, followed at 50 m horizontal distance by two other pilots. The wind is nil, and you begin an approach circuit to the left with the final phase toward the west. Upon entering base you discover that there is actually a wind of 2 to 5 km/h from the east, and therefore:

options.A: you continue with the chosen circuit, but you anticipate that your trajectory on the final phase will have a lower slope.
options.B: you immediately interrupt the chosen circuit, heading toward the leeward zone of the spot, and you land into the wind.
options.C: you continue with the chosen circuit, but you anticipate that your trajectory on the final phase will have a steeper slope.
options.D: you take the final phase as base, and you ultimately land with a crosswind.

q367.question: You arrive second at the landing zone, with distances of 50 m horizontally relative to the first and the third. There is a gentle wind from the west (2 to 5 km/h) and there are no obstacles in the vicinity. The first pilot begins an approach circuit to the right, and plans to land with a tailwind. You:

options.A: follow the pilot ahead of you, making the same approach circuit to avoid the risk of collision.
options.B: make an approach circuit to the right, but anticipating a landing into the wind.
options.C: make an approach circuit to the left and also land with a tailwind.
options.D: make an approach circuit to the left and land into the wind.

q368.question: You are making your approach circuit at a landing where the windsock indicates a gentle breeze from the west. On final you discover that the wind has shifted and now comes from the east at 5 to 10 km/h intensity. You:

options.A: immediately make a 180-degree turn and land into the wind.
options.B: continue the path as planned, flying more braked to compensate for the tailwind and reduce ground speed.
options.C: continue the path as planned, sit up, fly without braking, and pull the brakes for the landing a bit earlier.
options.D: continue the path as planned and, by means of successive and regular pulls on both brakes, try to make the wing lose altitude vertically.

options.A: the trajectory has a lower slope and the landing speed is lower.
options.B: the trajectory has a steeper slope and the landing speed is higher.
options.C: the trajectory has a lower slope and the landing speed is higher.
options.D: the trajectory has a steeper slope and the landing speed is lower.

options.A: the trajectory has a lower slope and the landing speed is lower.
options.B: the trajectory has a steeper slope and the landing speed is higher.
options.C: the trajectory has a lower slope and the landing speed is higher.
options.D: the trajectory has a steeper slope and the landing speed is lower.

q371.question: You land into a headwind of 30 km/h. As soon as your feet touch the ground:

options.A: you pull the brakes down as far as you can so that the wing immediately stops flying.
options.B: you turn 180 degrees and pull the brakes down as far as you can so that the wing immediately stops flying.
options.C: you pull one brake as far as you can so that the wing falls onto the leading edge and thereby stops providing lift.
options.D: you turn 180 degrees and pull the B or C risers so that the wing immediately stops flying.
